C[yuri: sitting at the studio, my wife is packing up sculptures in a huff, and I'm trying to learn Dlang for no good reason
C[garlicsoup: Dare I ask why she's packing them?

yuri: oh, I asked, its not completely clear. being that we're artists we have money trouble..actually its more complicated than that. we live in sf and pay rent on a house and a studio. we're trying to figure out how to cut costs, but we don't have a plan. so I think she's just 'doing something'. she keeps talking about just needing two bags, but her tools and materials and sculpture would easily fill a small truck...so idk
C[garlicsoup: Read you loud and clear buddy, COL in the Bay is astronomical
garlicsoup: have you considered moving to the wilderness
garlicsoup: moving to a junkyard in New Jersey and making sculptures out of trash

C[yuri: yeah. like I said, its complicated. she has high standards of living, and I'm actually a software engineer..but I can't seem to hold down a job because I'm a real old software engineer, that used to design supercomputers, and I can't tolerate this useless devops environment. so yeah, dropping out is the cards, but its tempting to keep trying
C[yuri: never tried online-go therapy before
C[garlicsoup: On a bit of a binge myself.
C[garlicsoup: That's very intriguing, that you have the computer experience.
garlicsoup: My dad keeps asking me to explain why it's Silicon valley but I'm not really clear on it myself.
garlicsoup: Do they actually have silicon there? i don't really think so. And how does the silicon actually work
garlicsoup: and can you play go with it
C[garlicsoup: Anyhow I'm sorry that you two are in that predicament, I hope everything works out for the better
yuri: its kinda fine. we've just been able to do art and live comfortably and now we have to face the same problems everyone else has :) but thank you
C[garlicsoup: Good to be aware and grateful. The funny thing is we all still have emotions, ups and downs and so forth.
C[garlicsoup: This game got heated in a hurry, ha ha
garlicsoup: Must be that early evening bright Mars appearance.

Arion: I visited your web site and enjoyed your sculpture.  Sculpture is a hobby of mine.
C[Arion: I visited your web site and enjoyed your sculpture.  Sculpture is a hobby of mine.
C[yuri: It’s actually my wife’s. I do some of the support work and patinas. What kind of work do you do?
C[Arion: I'm a software architect, but my wife and I have broad interest in all things creative. Come visit our website blackblocks.org
C[yuri: I'm actually a quasi-retired software architect myself
C[Arion: Really!  wow, maybe you should visit Synario.com, the software I've been working on for the past 25 years

C[yuri: looks pretty nice! I feel like these 'workbench' systems have really gotten short shrift up until I guess python notebooks.  I work almost entirely at the lower levels - OSs, compilers, distributed sytems
yuri: I think I really screwed up by not trying to bootstrap a long term effort like that, I bets its been a struggle though
C[Arion: What does quasi-retired mean ... consulting? contracting?

C[yuri: well, I did a database startup circa 2008 and kinda left the industry and started doing art metal fabrication, which is how I met my wife, but I kept picking up contracts to pay the bills. the metal work has definitely tailed off and I've been trying to get back in the insdustry...my original background is HPC, so I thought I wold able to make some traction with this AI stuff...but its all devops - they just throw more bodies at the problems instead of solving them
yuri: so..I've had several full time positions in a row where we just couldn't have a reasonable conversation about what needed to be done

yuri: which in most cases is nothing - I used to be principal architect at Cray, so when there was say a driver problem I would just fix it, or talk to other people about fixing it. now I'm just 'well, you don't have the source or the documentation, so idk what you expect me to do about it, file a ticket with the vendor'
yuri: people just don't know how the game is played anymore )
Arion: I hear you and it sounds like what we go through as well. We just went through a 4 week outside audit of our SDLC process
column2: I'm hoping this leads to improvements.

column1: yuri: how big is your shop?
C[yuri: testing for me is the big differentiator
Arion: We have 5 people covering software, qa and devops. And then we another 15 people in sales, markeing, and service.
C[yuri: you at least _have_ a qa person or persons

C[Arion: LOL ... when we merged with PFM I was the only person doing all technical stuff and our new partners asked who we should hire and I said the first and most important person to hire was a qa. They just stared at me
column2: they had no idea so I had to explain why!

guided: i don't see a way to capture any part of the M7 group yet, but black's been forced to put a bunch of inefficient moves in (not that my stones around O6 are working very hard either)
guided: but the H6 group seems imperiled
C[guided: ok, now we can capture a chunk of the group on the right if black doesn't put in a move to save it, and the group on the left looks completely dead if black doesn't play there now
C[guided: well, there's a semeai developing now, because my group isn't independently alive on the lower right
guided: keep an eye on it
C[guided: this situation is tough to read, but i've found a way to connect in each variation i've read here. F3 is a good response to most of black's moves, and if black takes F3 we are OK too.

C[guided: it's possible i could have turned this into a more large-scale fight for the whole corner too, but it seemed risky with the E5 stone potentially escaping into the center and threatening to break the J6 stones free as well
guided: and even if black's corner stones survive this is a very profitable exchange if i capture the rest
C[guided: next question: is there any threat to escape here based on the E8 stone??
C[guided: i'm reasonably sure black cannot escape using the E8 stone, given various threats i have to make small captures to expand liberties and/or live on the lower side
guided: so it seems a priority to stop black from making a large left side territory
C[guided: one possible outcome: submit black to a 3rd line position on the left while cutting off the E8 stone
guided: other possibilities seem likely to lead to more intense fighting
guided: i declined to invite more complexity immediately by playing the hane
C[guided: well, things got very messy
guided: i may need to do something to ensure that my groups on the lower side actually live
C[guided: ha! that's just about the cleverest ladder breaker i've ever seen
guided: i'd like to play F18 but then i lose my ladder
C[guided: yeah i don't think this is working....
C[guided: if the O7 group can live it's worth continuing. not clear yet. it's certainly in peril.
guided: too bad that ko fight wasn't actually a ko fight. E1 is a really good threat, heh.
guided: F8 is going to go down as one of the worst moves i've ever made
C[guided: H12 breaks the ladder but i have more important fish to fry
C[guided: seems like i'm pretty thoroughly screwed.
guided: likely will resign pretty soon
C[guided: keep crawling i guess. may be able to actually threaten some black stones, depending
guided: gg, thanks for the game
